item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. I'm a sucker for the more psychological horrors, like SCP-3001, the ones that use emotion to fuck with you. It's amazing, and it's honestly chilling in a nice way.

As a reader, I'd personally want Blue to be right: that the entire anomaly is just an anomalous divider between the groups. But in reality, it's like Model Red, because otherwise…

But you can't know. No one does. And if Ehwez tries to figure it out, it could spell completely disaster.

The line that best sums this up is this:

We could be sitting on a landmine, or on a cushion, and either way it would really be a poor idea to get up right now.

My only complaint is the Model Blue and Model Red coloring in the object class is a bit unfriendly (the rest of the colored text has context). I'd add a small footnote to the Object Class, but that's it.

Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm pretty late to this, but — if your response is " it's really sounding like you didn't actually read this" it's far more likely that you didn't do a good enough job making your idea understandable.

I've read through the page, and your comments here, a couple of times now — and I still just don't get it.

I understand the basic premise — but not the narrative that emerges from it, which leaves me with the same objections others have made. Part of it is that there is no attempt to clarify how, exactly, interactions between the groups work. That could be accomplished with an experiment log, or some observational notes… But there's nothing like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not fond of this. First, the entire introduction before the "+ Show ERROR_NO_TITLE_GIVEN (Clearance Level ERROR:INVALID_CLEAR_ID Required.)" set me up for a completely different piece than the one that I got. I was expecting an SCP dealing with the meta of reused designations or contaminations because of the charts and bolding, but as it turns out the SCP has nothing to do with this. I enjoyed reading the Previous Item Assignments because it was funny, but it's true that you could remove it all and have only the "+ Show ERROR_NO_TITLE_GIVEN (Clearance Level ERROR:INVALID_CLEAR_ID Required.)" and have the context for the rest of the story stand fine on its own. This also creates a kind of mood whiplash because you have the lighthearted introduction contrasted with the highly melodramatic SHOULD WE NUKE THEM ALL.

+ show block
– hide block

Next, the Model Red/Model Blue categorizations just end up really, really dumb when they're all they mean is are they people/are they not people. "a large controlling intelligence coordinates the Out-Group" is a stupid conclusion to draw when they only evidence they have is well it's unlikely to have happened by chance. Just off the top of my head— why would they assume the anomaly is something controlling the out-group, when the anomaly could be the rift itself, or an effect that affects discrete individuals, such as people with a like gene avoid each other? Why would they immediately jump to the most conspiracy theory answer possible?

And then, of course, to the jump that the Evil Intelligence means that we must murder them. It reads like an alt-right piece, honestly, that we don't know anything about these people or what they want so we must kill them before they maybe kill us.

[Maybe they're a collective illusion. We have no idea what they do when they aren't around us, they might not even exist as soon as they leave our sight. We don't know if we can trust anything they say, if anything they consider objective reality is true. We don't know anything about them because if we knew them they'd be us.]
Except for the thing where they're scientists and entirely built around testing things to see if they're true or not. They "might not even exist as soon as they leave our sight" is solipsistic nonsense— this is always true and applies to everyone in all groups. "We don't know if we can trust anything they say, if anything they consider objective reality is true.", um, no, if one of them says it's raining you can totally look outside and see if they're right or not.

[They completely staff no fewer than eighteen Foundation Sites- hell, they're two thirds of the human-derived SCPs.]
Also, this points out a problem with the setup. The Foundation works based off of communication and has out-groups writing SCPs. They describe their own lives, towns, and anomalous phenomena, things that are independently verifiable. Oh, the out-group says there's a closed portal in this location or that they amnesized this town after removing the cockroaches from their brain stems— why don't we go over there and see if the portal-closing markers are there and if the people have surgery scars? Why don't we follow some of those researchers around and see if their life matches up to their transcript if we're curious if they're people, or if we're really scared of an intelligence making it all up, why don't we install cameras everywhere to try to trick it into being seen or go through the out-group archive to see if anything doesn't match up. If the problem is communication, they can still read our articles and we can still read theirs— we could tell them about the divide and have a meeting about what to do about it? And if they live like people, talk like people, and stop anomalies like people, why are we still assuming that they're secretly not people? The Foundation implicitly works on trust— we're sharing these articles around so people will know what to do with these very dangerous things. If the other group of people secretly want to kill us, why are we telling them about exactly where and how the unkillable reptillian superweapon is stored? Why are we scared about them attacking us with nukes and their greater population when their most dangerous weapons are right under all our noses?

What might improve this is if the Foundation was entirely in-group, instead of the two-thirds thing. The Foundation is already kind of a secret society and the ridiculous paranoia would make more sense since they're both the people dealing very directly with terrible world-destroying forces and also are separated from much of society, so the whole "63% Nay, 34% Aye, and 3% abstaining from voting" thing makes more sense than 34% of people just deciding they want to genocide everyone else including the coworkers that they see every morning.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the concept and execution, buuut …
It's one of those pieces that's so clever that the flaws feel glaring.

First, there's one vital piece of data that should be trivial to learn, but isn't stated: what is the most recent date at which the effect could have begun. Assuming heredity and/or de facto family are "familiar," genealogy should give some REALY precise data points. Not only could you figure out when (if in recorded history) the effect began, but you could find out what, if any, cross-population adoptions took place. This would be very important information that the MTF should have. Even if the effect is so old that this can't be ascertained, THAT would be important, because it would show how stable the current state of affairs is. Oh, and -just realized- this would also allow tracking of the ratio of in-group and out-group over time!

Second, they're in the Foundation. They've seen more than enough weirdness that 2 options is ridiculously limited. Amusingly, the single most likely answer to me is that this SCP is actually a containment for a different socially propagating SCP, or a Foundation created firebreak to keep infohazards in check.

So, congratulations on making something so cool I can't upvote it because the little things are bugging me too much! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The opening is a lot of pointless fluff obfuscating an otherwise stellar work. The same effect of deterring unauthorized access could be done in any number of ways that doesn't detract from what follows.

I do, however, understand the decision to keep it despite complaints — it is an interesting and somewhat unique take on what an unused slot might appear as, so much so that it's something I don't think should be lost entirely.

I believe that it could easily be removed from this and reworked such that it franes an entirely different story — one tied to the recycling of the slot, such that it's put to good use as opposed to essentially being a large waste of time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yet another collosal mindfuck. Thanks
+1

Edit: I also like the implication that 3125 might be the something that controls the out group being a huge fan of that scip and the antimemetics division.

Or am I just drawing false connections here?

item.title: Huh
item.content:encoded:
I don't see any references to 4694…

Edit: Ah, there it is. I would imagine it probably doesn't mean anything. I mean, the article also contains a reference to SCP-3125, but clearly doesn't have anything to do with 3125, Antimemetics, or Fifthism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cool to see SCP-4694 make a cameo appearance! Is there a significance there that hasn't been picked up on?

Asking for a friend.

+1 BTW

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think paranoia is the theme of this article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Had to read this a couple times but I absolutely love it. Now I know to definitely not talk to strangers
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thing is, if model blue is true, the out-group might as well have the exact same hypothesis about the in-group, without either side knowing it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a red herring, essentially. Because Ehwaz-1 needs *some* secrecy but not a great deal, they're squatting on an unused SCP-number. The top text and the first two dropdowns is just showing what an empty article listing might look like in-universe. It's irrelevant to the rest of the article, it just needs to be there so Ehwaz-1 has somewhere to hide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They control two thirds of the O5 council.

This one stands out like a sore thumb while reading. I would say it's almost impossible for the O5 to not be part of the same group by virtue of working together, whether they're familiar with each other's private life (if they even have one) or not. It would be much more effective if the article stated that all of the O5 council is Out-Group.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Felt like I was reading a real paper on the subject and not an Scp I loved the Idea I cannot grasp how good you are at making up new ideas!
Would +2 if I could
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can someone smarter than me explain what the point of the first two drop-downs are? Are they just a misdirection thing for main article, because that does seem pretty unnecessary? I really liked the final letter, even if it does essentially re-state the rest of the article since I was a bit confused about a few things.

+1'ed, slightly apprehensively since I'm really trying to work out what the purpose of those first two drop-downs.

item.title: Mind Fuckery Done Right
item.content:encoded:
I seldom see a scip that messes with your mind by basing itself in an otherwise ridiculous concept that, under the right conditions, really resonates with you.

This is one of those.

On an added note, why all the invisible I's in the blank space?

item.title: +1 obviously
item.content:encoded:
What. The. Heck.
Thank you. Now I will not stop thinking of who are these Out-Group people for the rest of the year.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the "designation available for assignment" part, it feels really in line with the SCP Foundation, with precautions about the safety of that particular SCP number (even across multiverse!), and information about previous designations. Also showing the foundation isn't perfect when information is lost in containment breaches!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is absolutely amazing and quite thought-provoking. The idea is quite simple, but it explored in such interesting ways and presented fantastically. My only complaint is that the opening goes on for too long, without any concrete connection to the rest of the article.

Regardless, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely loved this article it has to be my new favorite I love the idea and the issues that it presents and the way you convey two opposite ideals is amazing.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so I'm positively in love with this article. This is one of those skips that I yearn to discover 90% of my time on this website. This is an SCP-2000, or a 3002 or 3519: an article that takes the speculative nature of SCP and uses it to pose a systemic problem and make a real-world examination of that problem. It's so unique, and so well-thought out.

This makes the Out-Group sounds so alien — even if they're humans like the rest of us, they are a degree of separation too distant to properly identify with on a social level. Maybe they aren't the problem — maybe we are. This makes Ehwaz seem desperate in a fight tipped against them from the start. One that they've lost merely by existing (and don't think I didn't see that off-hand SCP-3125 reference — intentional or otherwise!). Their worldview depends on this model, and to test or bolster that worldview could also shatter it.

All these things said, I have to point to the "Note At The End" trope as the only part I'm not entirely thrilled by. Granted, I enjoy this all the same and don't think it should be cut down! While the note re-explains information the reader should know after reading the rest of the skip, I do enjoy its exploration into Ehwaz as a group of their own. I like the implications of how the world would look in a Model Red situation — something you don't get in the main article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fascinating idea, largely well executed, though I admit I sped through some parts. The bit I'm hung up on is where you state "Nobody in the In-Group can identify a member of the Out-Group," though earlier it was stated that many famous people are members of the out group. I'm just trying to figure out what you mean by "identify" and how that would play out. I'm picturing a situation, like, an In-Group member can't think of the name of, for example, the leader of a foreign country, or the founder of a large company, who in this case is a member of the Out-Group. Is there some force in place that would stop them (other than fear of forcing a breach) from learning about that person, what they look like, details of their life, by looking that information up online? Does that count as "identifying"? Does it not constitute a breach, though, because the familiarity necessarily would only be one way? Either way, I think it's not the clearest phrasing.

item.title: Re: Birth
item.content:encoded:
I don't think it's really a genetic thing. For it to be so, we would first have to assume that the out-group actually exists, which it very well may not. They could be illusions. If the people in it do exist, then both Model Blue and Model Red would ensure that none of their offspring would ever fall into the hands of the In-group. With Model Blue, there is some inexplicable quirk of nature/reality/pure chance itself that is keeping them separate. With Model Red, there is something or someone that's forcibly keeping them apart.

In either case, no one born into the in-group would ever have a chance to be adopted by anyone in the out-group since they almost never meet, especially not in hospitals or adoption centers, and nobody in the out-group would ever be allowed to fall into the hands of the in-group, even accidentally. I would imagine if people in the out-group are real and are being controlled somehow, a baby from the out-group that truly, inexplicably through some unimaginable series of events fell into the hands of someone in the in-group without there being some direct breach, it could always just kill them, or abandon them to the in-group, but I get the feeling that's never happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I misread the entry and thought it was indicating the Out-Group might not know about the In-Group.

That being said: Unless the In-Group instinctively avoids the Out-Group or this is just due to a probability anomaly, they should still be making attempts to breach the divide without even knowing it. Even if the Out-Group actively avoids familiarity, the In-Group won't know to and so will be randomly approaching members of the Out-Group.

Attempts to do that by members of Ehwaz shouldn't draw any particular attention unless they're normally introverted. But if you're the kind of person who is already outgoing, it should be easy to mask as ordinary friendliness. Correspondingly, if the Red Model is correct and you're ordinarily the sort of person who's very extroverted, uniformly avoiding the Out-Group would potentially tip them off.

Actually, now that I think about it—it should be possible to tell whether or not the Red Model is correct based on observation, without any attempts from Ehwaz to gain familiarity directly. Watch members of the In-Group interact in an office setting where you've identified what group everyone is in. If they don't make any attempts at gaining familiarity with members of the Out-Group, it's probably a statistical anomaly. If they do but members of the Out-Group uniformly deny their attempts, then the Red Model is probably correct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing is, if there's a single intelligence behind the Out-Group, it should already realize that none of the people it's puppeting know any of the In-Group.

It presumably does, and it's presumably maintaining isolation on purpose. Why is a different question.

Also, what's stopping there from being more than two groups?

Absolutely nothing at all. They can't even guarantee that Out-Group people exist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing is, if there's a single intelligence behind the Out-Group, it should already realize that none of the people it's puppeting know any of the In-Group. The In-Group figured this out as soon as they mapped relationships—if the Red Model is correct, the Out-Group should be able to do the same very easily. Especially since the NIES technology, based on the information given, should be available to members of the Out-Group.

Also, what's stopping there from being more than two groups? The Out-Group's social relationships haven't been mapped; for all the MTF knows the Out-Group acts like a buffer separating multiple In-Groups.

item.title: Re: Birth
item.content:encoded: People that have never gotten to know their biological parents perhaps? Then would they be "genetically" out but mentally in?

item.title: Birth
item.content:encoded:
I would imagine any child born to an in-group member would be an in-group member itself by default. How could they not be? They're conceived and raised by in-group members and start forming social connections with them at a very early age.

This is especially true if Model Red is the correct behavior model and the out-group people are illusions or part of some hive mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very strong concept well, executed, and a pretty easy upvote. I do have a couple issues, the main one being, as others have stated, the vague nature of "familiarity." I feel like this needs to be explored and clarified more deeply. What, for instance, happens when a member of one group gives birth to a member of the other?

The note at the end certainly feels tacked on, but honestly for me it helped clarify exactly what was going on, so I think it should stay.

Like I said, fantastic concept with horrifying implications. I would like to see other articles and tales that expand on this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it's perfectly okay to do rewrites after you've put the article up, especially if it's something that majorly improves the article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's really that hard to believe that a model red puppetmaster wouldn't be able to do this with actual people? Seriously?

If it exerts 24/7 control on the people (and it would more or less have to in order to do what it does) then the boundary between killing them and releasing them from a system of constant slavery is blurred to hell, certainly to the point where it is no worse than other Foundation standard procedures.

If the intelligence is so hypercompetent that making small talk with a guard on a business trip is enough for it to figure out people know it exists, it's absurd that it wouldn't already know about Ehwaz.

I don't want to be rude but it's really sounding like you didn't actually read this because this entire point is specifically addressed. The system can't be breached by accident or it would already have been breached millions of times over. Therefore even if the intelligence knew everything that the Out-Group knew, even if it somehow was the entire Out-Group, then it wouldn't know about Ehwaz because the Out-Group can never accidentally come across Ehwaz.

On the other hand if a member of a hive mind is suddenly contacted from beyond the hive mind for the first time ever it is at least extremely likely that some metaphorical eyebrows would be raised, and at that point we're back to crossing our fingers and hoping that it's not evil.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can only say that you should read it carefully. Model red "people" aren't people. They're either a shared nonexistent illusion or being abstractly controlled more or less constantly by some hive mind. They can't just be "people who don't know us", that is so ludicrously improbable it's not worth considering.

It's really that hard to believe that a model red puppetmaster wouldn't be able to do this with actual people? Seriously?

If you did this then the intelligence would be breached and become aware. If you make a breach and the intelligence is hostile then it will immediately know (it must know almost everything that happens to its subjects by definition in order to allow this ridiculously improbable thing to happen) and at that point you're crossing your fingers and hoping that this hive mind with five billion bodies at its command is friendly.

If the intelligence is so hypercompetent that making small talk with a guard on a business trip is enough for it to figure out people know it exists, it's absurd that it wouldn't already know about Ehwaz.

item.title: Hmm
item.content:encoded:
I felt that the meaning of "familiarity" was pretty obvious. It means knowing someone well enough to be connected to their part of the overall social network. It's more than merely knowing their name or occupation, but knowing the details of their life, who their friends are, how those friends fit in the social network. It involves a information-carrying connections between individuals and groups of individuals.

Meetings between In and Outgroup individuals are infrequent and the information flow in such situations is minimal at best. I would imagine the only time they ever come into contact is when whatever is controlling the outgroup decides it is absolutely necessary. I doubt it reveals any meaningful information about the outgroup. Definitely not enough to know any of them, and definitely not enough to know how they relate to other people in their group.

I also have to wonder how they could go about testing this in a safe or effective way. If Model Blue is true, the fact that this phenomenon hasn't been accidentally breached by now, especially now that we know it's there, means that any attempt to test it will almost surely inexplicably fail. If model red is true, and whatever is causing this to happen isn't benevolent, it could mean disaster, as it controls 2/3 of the world's population. It's just not worth the risk.

They have to operate on conjecture and assumptions because they really have no idea how to proceed. The letter at the end even admits that it could indeed be absolutely nothing, but until they find a way to get more information in a discrete manner, they're right to assume there could be something sinister about it. After all, they live in a world rife with all manner of malevolent reality and mind-bending abominations, as well as numerous groups with anomalous members and objects all vying for power and influence. IMO, their belief that it could be a conspiracy is absolutely justified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this article has a very strong premise, but doesn’t develop it properly.

+ Show
- Hide

For starters, the entire anomaly hinges on the concept of familiarity, but the article fails to explain what is meant with this term. It’s also extremely hard to extrapolate it from the text, since we don’t get to see the anomaly at work directly.
Then we are introduced to the two “Models”, which are, in my opinion, fairly ludicrous: the first it’s just an objective summary of what is known of the anomaly, while the second is a literal conspiracy theory hanging on baseless assumptions. Neither one of these has any real basis, to be fair, since there hasn’t been any real testing on the anomaly for… reasons? And then we get the nuclear option, which is probably meant to be a bad idea in-universe, but comes out of nothing and ends in nothing done.


-1

(Also, the entire format screw is a bit unnecessary, but it didn’t influence my vote)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Some fair points here. Formatting and opening aren't for everyone.

— Many of the members of the out-group in model red are actual people who have committed no wrongdoing or awareness of the phenomenon even if the cause has.

I can only say that you should read it carefully. Model red "people" aren't people. They're either a shared nonexistent illusion or being abstractly controlled more or less constantly by some hive mind. They can't just be "people who don't know us", that is so ludicrously improbable it's not worth considering.

— None of the out group in model red have done anything to justify thermonuclear strikes. What are we, the fucking GOC?

That's a valid viewpoint and a good reason for two-thirds of Ehwaz-1 to vote against it. Counterpoint, though: An overhwelming majority of the human population being fake and/or controlled by an unknown hive mind (which is for some reason dedicated to concealing its own existence) is automatically threatening by its very nature.

— The members of the out group in model red are immune to thermonuclear strikes

There's no way of determining that for sure, which is why the plan also includes several SCP objects.

f you could maneuver a new level of clearance and unauthorized use of a powerful tool you can find a reason to go to one of those sites on business and just fucking try to schmooze.

If you did this then the intelligence would be breached and become aware. If you make a breach and the intelligence is hostile then it will immediately know (it must know almost everything that happens to its subjects by definition in order to allow this ridiculously improbable thing to happen) and at that point you're crossing your fingers and hoping that this hive mind with five billion bodies at its command is friendly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh.
The premise doesn't click with me, and that's not even getting into all the little things that bother me.

- The opening is totally pointless and a waste of time.
- The flouting of standard formatting for no good reason (the colors, the subsections, etc.)
- For a mostly unknown phenomenon, much of the article takes an extremely black-and-white view of the anomaly's cause, with no attempt to consider alternatives. Either it's a natural phenomenon that cannot be broken (unless 'we're wrong' in some unexplained way) or it's the boogeyman in the out-group keeping it from happening but it somehow can't stop it from happening by force? (That one really confuses me. Why would it being caused by something somehow make it possible to breach?) Like, let me fire off some alternatives,
— It's literally just a massive coincidence.
— The 'in group' is the source, which would make sense considering it's smaller.
— Many of the members of the out-group in model red are actual people who have committed no wrongdoing or awareness of the phenomenon even if the cause has.
— None of the out group in model red have done anything to justify thermonuclear strikes. What are we, the fucking GOC?
— The members of the out group in model red are immune to thermonuclear strikes
- Seriously? The best way you can think of to breach the effect is by forcing someone to tell you about their kids at gunpoint? Christ, you work at the same organization with entire sites full of the out-group, if you could maneuver a new level of clearance and unauthorized use of a powerful tool you can find a reason to go to one of those sites on business and just fucking try to schmooze.

Long story short, a no-brainer -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who studies in group/out group behavior, it was really cool to see such a great SCP discussing it. :D

I've been considering doing something similar for a while, and it looks like I'll have to up my game!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot. There's stuff on this site that's very down-to-earth and stuff like this that's very conceptual. It's harder to make the conceptual stuff work, but I think it's very rewarding when it does.

Easy +1

item.title: Brilliant
item.content:encoded: Interesting, thought-provoking, and more than a little creepy. This is easily one of my favorite skips ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I almost backed out of it before I clicked on the last drop-down.
Sure glad I didn't. Thanks for fucking with everyone, and everything.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I do not understand how you people come up with these, let alone communicate them in a clear and entertaining manner.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is exactly the kind of article that I absolutely love this site for engendering. A weird idea, taken to its logical extreme, then dressed up in science talk that reveals just enough horror to plant that seed of dread.

Excellent job. Thank you for your work!

+ minor notes
- nitpicks ahoy!
  • I really liked the decoy at the start: personally I wouldn't change a thing about the "this slot is empty" sections.
  • You misspelled "separate" basically every time. Do a find/replace for "seperate"
  • The explanation of the phenomenon is probably some of the most cerebral stuff I've read on this site: lots of concepts-within-concepts talk. For the most part it works, but if you do some editing that's the section I'd focus on tightening up.
  • I agree with the others that the end letter is too long, but I think we need something. Right now it's not giving us the benefit of the doubt that we've read the article: the letter should only include new info, rather than rehashing the old stuff. A perfect example of this is your final line, which is a perfect "button" to end on because it summarizes the situation in a new way: "We could be sitting on a landmine, or on a cushion, and either way it would really be a poor idea to get up right now."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted to have a previous designation for the SCP to make Ehwaz feel a bit more ramshackle and trying to skirt around notice. 3125 was the first example I could find of an SCP where the designation is relevant to the article, a case where the Ehwaz team could have been forced to move their SCP to 4857 because someone really needed a specific slot for their SCP. 2317 would also have worked but that's a huge, well-known article and I didn't want people being disappointed when there was no real link (it's also one of my absolute favourites). 3125 is very good but I feel a bit less well known.

The summary letter is indeed a bit shit, but it's up now and I dont think people would take kindly to a rewrite within 24 hrs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if its a meme that affects the "In Group"? spoopy

Anyway, like most other people here, I feel that the end note kills the article. Its a bit like that summary book report you have to fill out in school. You could have trimmed it down. Apart from that, very interesting article! I love the idea. I must say Im a blue man myself :)

Oh and do the former skips mean anything I wonder… 3125 caught my attention.

Ima quit rambling, just take the upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd really like to see a whole spin-off foundation verse built on this concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You wanna know something else uncertain that makes this all more disturbing?
We don't know if the out-group are the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fantastic.

The Out-Group must not be made aware that it has been detected at any cost.

I mean, this sentence by itself is a perfect example of how you hook.

But the whole thing is great because it's one of those big "is it or isn't it?" things the Foundation can never truly know about. The hush-hush "you're MTF now" setup is well executed, but more important is that central ethical question that looms over the entire discussion. And like… this is xenophobia, racism, the gross tribalism that underpins every single aspect of human society. This is big!

I'd like to think that because they've been dithering for thirty years, Method Blue is in fact correct, but can you ever really be sure?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

the bolding is a formatting thing. The bold sections are the parts of the boilerplate which are specific to 4857

I'm aware, and it's weirdly cool to see what an empty slot looks like. I can't imagine the bolding would look that way, though; it seems suspicious, which is what you're trying to avoid.

yup, but they're what I feel an actual empty SCP slot would look like.

I'm just confused about what it brings to the story, either through narrative or theme. It would be a cool place to emphasize deception and paranoia as a motif, what with all the redactions, but nothing listed is intriguing enough to get me paranoid and the article doesn't seem to capitalize on either theme.

I feel I can say that's a personal thing. I can probably find worse things they've done.

I'm not one to shy away from the Foundation doing bad things; most of the articles and tales I write are about just that (SCP-4051, SCP-4415, Systems Patch). It just seems out-of-character for a Veil-breaking operation to be considered when the threat presented is so low/ambiguous.

I'm totally down for this idea, it's just that the execution doesn't work for me. You do you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't have a issue with non-standard classes, don't get me wrong. I just feel there's a better and more formal way to announce the duality instead of "Euclid/Keter", especially when the colors haven't been defined yet. I believe kaktus had a multi-class SCP somewhere that worked well…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
• the bolding is a formatting thing. The bold sections are the parts of the boilerplate which are specific to 4857
  • yup, but they're what I feel an actual empty SCP slot would look like. Some record keeping about previous items to fill the slot and some documentation saying whether or not the slot has been compromised in some way
  • I feel they work, but you probably have a point
  • my bad
  • also my bad, may edit something
  • I feel I can say that's a personal thing. I can probably find worse things they've done.
  • yeah it's the weakest part of the whole thing. I got a lot of people who didn't understand it without it though so idk.
  • also also my bad

Thanks for the comment, I'll bear it in mind if I do a rewrite

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with mostof your points, excluding the 'dual class' and 'colouration of text' points.

In regards to the dual classing - the colours show that the class of the anomaly varies on which Model turns out to be true; if Blue Model is true, it's Euclid. If Red Model, it's a Keter.

In regards to the colouration, I'll admit its mostly redundant since it's only applied to 'Model Red'/'Model Blue' text - but it also clarifies my above 'dual classing' point.

As my own quip, I'm unconvinced this file needs this much effort to be concealed - wouldn't there be a way to abuse the anomaly to ensure infosec, if the anomaly didn't automatically hide the file from Out-Group to preserve the gap?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [This was needlessly hostile and I apologize. Dunno why I reacted the way I did; you're doing good.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was absolutely stunning up until the literal "note at the end", which put rather a damper on things by basically recapping everything you already told the reader in the description. Trim that down (or find a different way to convey the information) and you've got a decent article — otherwise, you've got a fairly bloated concept that falls into all the same potholes as the various other "hidden information" scips. No-vote for now :/
When I critted this draft, I wasn’t really a fan. However, the author has responded to basically all of my issues and I feel happy upvoting this now. Well done!

item.title: Hopefully this time goes a little better.
item.content:encoded:
Or maybe it won't. Who knows?

This image used to make the Ehwaz-1 logo (the non-Foundation bit) is borrowed from:

Original Source: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Social_Network_Analysis_Visualization.png
Creator: Grandjean, Martin (2014).

The Ehwaz in the logo is just an Ehwaz. I dont think I took it from anywhere and if I did it's just 4 lines.

I think I got the creator correct there.

Edit: Now this hasn't tanked (god willing) I'm going to thank people. Didn't do it before because nobody wants to be associated with a sinking ship. Roll credits!

Thanks to:
LilyFlower
AbsentmindedNihilist
gee0765
LadyKatie
DrAkimoto
KadeLeMuse
UraniumEmpire
Rehevkor
Bluedanoob
Metaphysician
T Rutherford
Bunton
Nanoro

Also probably other people who posted on critters and not PMs so I can't find your names.

Edit 2: Holy shit it broke 100. Thanks so much, and double thanks to everyone who helped.
